title: Poisoning — General Approach
oneLiner: Life-threatening systemic disturbance from xenobiotic exposure requiring systematic ABC assessment, decontamination, targeted antidotes, and supportive care; common in self-harm and accidental ingestion.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 22-year-old woman is brought to resus by ambulance after being found at home with an empty bottle of co-codamol and a note. She is drowsy (GCS 10), pupils are pin-point (1–2 mm), respiratory rate is 6 breaths/min with oxygen saturations of 88% on air. Heart rate is 50 bpm, blood pressure 90/60. She does not respond to loud voice but withdraws to pain. A friend confirms she had been depressed and took “about 20 pills” two hours ago.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- Young adult with deliberate self-harm – most common demographic
- Empty bottle of co-codamol – combination of paracetamol (hepatotoxic) + codeine (opioid)
- Drowsy, GCS 10 – CNS depression from opioid or co-ingestant
- Pinpoint pupils (miosis) – opioid receptor activation (mu-agonist)
- Respiratory rate 6, hypoxia – opioid-induced respiratory depression (central chemoreceptor inhibition)
- Bradycardia, hypotension – opioid also causes vagal stimulation and peripheral vasodilation
- Withdrawal only to pain – moderate CNS depression (opioid plus potential paracetamol toxicity, but paracetamol does not cause early drowsiness)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Mixed overdose:** Patient may have tachycardia (anticholinergic) plus miosis (opioid) – confusing toxidrome. Always consider co-ingestion.
- **Delayed toxidrome:** Paracetamol overdose presents with NO symptoms for 24–48 hours, then right upper quadrant pain, hepatic failure. A patient who “feels fine” after a large ingestion may still be at risk.
- **Elderly accidental overdose:** Confusion, falls, polypharmacy – subtle signs (e.g., bradycardia from beta-blocker OD, hypoglycemia from sulfonylurea OD) easily attributed to age.
- **Unknown substance:** Don’t assume illicit drugs; consider household chemicals (ethylene glycol in antifreeze, organophosphates in pesticides) with metabolic acidosis and osmolar gap.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Poisoning is the harmful effect that occurs when a substance (drug, chemical, toxin) is ingested, inhaled, injected, or absorbed in a dose that exceeds the body’s ability to handle it. It can be intentional (self-harm, suicide) or accidental (childhood, occupational, iatrogenic).

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Poisoning accounts for > 200,000 ED attendances annually in the UK, with ~5000 deaths. It is the most common method of suicide in young adults.
- **Stakes:** Early recognition and escalation can be lifesaving – many poisonings have specific antidotes (e.g., naloxone for opioids, N-acetylcysteine for paracetamol, flumazenil for benzodiazepines). Missed diagnosis leads to irreversible organ damage (liver failure, renal failure, brain injury) or death.
- **Common pitfalls:** Over-reliance on history (patient may be unable or unwilling to give accurate info); missing co-ingestion (paracetamol in every overdose); forgetting to measure osmolar gap in metabolic acidosis; giving flumazenil in chronic benzodiazepine use (seizure risk).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:** Poisoning begins with absorption of the toxin. The rate depends on route (oral, inhaled, dermal), formulation (immediate vs sustained release), and stomach contents (food delays absorption). Once absorbed, distribution to tissues – lipophilic drugs (opioids, TCAs) cross the blood-brain barrier quickly → CNS effects. Metabolism occurs mainly in the liver (CYP450 enzymes); some agents require biotransformation to toxic metabolites (e.g., paracetamol → NAPQI via CYP2E1). Excretion is via kidneys (unchanged drug or metabolites). Toxicity occurs when the rate of absorption exceeds detoxification and elimination.

**Why each key clinical sign happens:**
- **Opioid respiratory depression:** Mu-receptor agonism in the brainstem (pre-Bötzinger complex) reduces sensitivity to CO₂ → decreased respiratory rate and tidal volume → hypoxia, hypercapnia.
- **Miosis (pinpoint pupils):** Opioid stimulation of the Edinger-Westphal nucleus (parasympathetic) → sphincter pupillae contraction. Exception: meperidine (pethidine) and some synthetic opioids may cause mydriasis.
- **Anticholinergic delirium (e.g., TCA, antihistamine):** Blockade of central muscarinic acetylcholine receptors → agitation, confusion, seizures, hyperthermia. Peripheral block → dry flushed skin, dilated pupils, urinary retention, tachycardia.
- **Cholinergic crisis (e.g., organophosphate):** Irreversible acetylcholinesterase inhibition → excess acetylcholine at muscarinic (SLUDGE: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI upset, Emesis) and nicotinic (muscle fasciculations, weakness, paralysis) receptors.
- **Metabolic acidosis with increased anion gap:** Common in methanol, ethylene glycol, salicylate, and metformin overdose – accumulation of organic acids due to toxin metabolism or impaired cellular respiration.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** In our vignette, the drowsy state with miosis and bradypnoea is classic opioid effect. Had she taken only paracetamol, she would have been asymptomatic at 2 hours. The combination explains both early (opioid) and delayed (paracetamol) pictures.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Examples | Key Stem Clue |
|----------|----------|---------------|
| **Deliberate self-harm (most common)** | Paracetamol, antidepressants, opioids, antipsychotics, benzodiazepines | “Empty pill bottles, history of depression, suicide note” |
| **Accidental – paediatric** | Iron tablets, paracetamol syrup, cleaning products, button batteries | “Toddler found with open container; vomiting, drooling, stridor” (button battery) |
| **Accidental – elderly** | Digoxin, warfarin, diuretics, insulin, beta-blockers | “Confused elderly patient on multiple medications; bradycardia, hypoglycaemia” |
| **Iatrogenic** | IV potassium, insulin, morphine, chemotherapy errors | “Inpatient given 10x dose of insulin; hypoglycaemic seizure” |
| **Environmental / occupational** | Carbon monoxide, organophosphates, ethylene glycol (antifreeze), methanol, snake venom, lead | “Found in enclosed space with headache, nausea, cherry-red lips” (CO); “Garage worker with cholinergic crisis” |
| **Recreational / illicit** | Cocaine, amphetamines, MDMA, GHB, synthetic cannabinoids | “Young adult at party with tachycardia, hyperthermia, agitation” (sympathomimetic); “Comatose with hypothermia” (GHB) |
| **Food poisoning** | Shellfish toxins (paralytic shellfish poisoning), botulism, scrombrotoxin | “Numbness, weakness after eating mussels” (PSP); “Diplopia, descending flaccid paralysis after canned food” (botulism) |

**Most common cause overall:** Paracetamol overdose (intentional or accidental) – accounts for ~40% of all poisonings in the UK.
sections.5. Investigations – What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring:** The **Rumack-Matthew nomogram** for paracetamol toxicity: treatment indicated if plasma concentration > 150 mg/L at 4 hours (or plotted on nomogram). For salicylate overdose, the **Done nomogram** (rarely used now, rely on clinical + level). For methanol/ethylene glycol, **osmolar gap** > 10 mOsm/L is suggestive; **anion gap metabolic acidosis** is confirmatory.

**Core tests table:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **ECG** | Rate, rhythm, QRS/QTc intervals | Toxicity of many drugs (TCAs, antipsychotics, digoxin, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers) | QRS > 100 ms → TCA toxicity; QTc > 500 ms → risk of torsades de pointes; sinus bradycardia → opioid, beta-blocker, CCB; tachycardia → sympathomimetic, anticholinergic |
| **Paracetamol level** | Serum concentration | Gold standard – delayed hepatotoxicity can be prevented if NAC given early | Plot on nomogram at 4h (or timed sample). Level above 150 mg/L at 4h → start NAC |
| **Salicylate level** | Serum salicylate concentration | Suspicion of aspirin overdose (tinnitus, hyperventilation, metabolic acidosis) | Level > 30 mg/dL therapeutic; > 50 mg/dL toxic; > 90 mg/dL severe. Use nomogram for single acute ingestion |
| **ABG** (arterial blood gas) | pH, bicarbonate, PaCO₂, lactate | Acid-base status; high anion gap metabolic acidosis suggests methanol, ethylene glycol, metformin, or salicylate | Anion gap = Na⁺ - (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻) > 12; osmolar gap = measured - calculated > 10 (methanol, ethylene glycol) |
| **Coagulation screen** | PT/INR | Paracetamol hepatotoxicity – INR correlates with severity | INR > 1.5 at 24h suggests liver injury; > 2.0 at 72h is ominous |
| **Glucose** | Blood sugar | Hypoglycemia from sulfonylurea, insulin, ethanol (also screen for Addison's in undifferentiated collapse) | Low glucose → IV dextrose; high glucose may be stress response |
| **Pregnancy test** (urine/blood β-hCG) | +/− | Pregnancy alters management – teratogenicity, altered metabolism, UGI motility (delayed absorption) | Always check in women of childbearing age; affects decisions on AC, ICU transfer |
| **Urine drug screen** (qualitative) | Presence of opioids, benzodiazepines, TCAs, cocaine metabolites | Adjunct when history is unreliable; may support toxidrome | Positive screen confirms exposure but does not quantitate; may miss synthetic opioids (fentanyl) |

**Pattern-recognition single findings:**
- **QRS > 100 ms with right axis deviation (R wave in aVR > 3 mm)** → TCA overdose until proven otherwise
- **Anion gap metabolic acidosis + osmolar gap** → methanol/ethylene glycol (start fomepizole or ethanol)
- **Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic acidosis** → salicylate toxicity (mixed acid-base disorder)
- **INR > 4.0 + bilirubin > 300 μmol/L + lactic acidosis** → fulminant hepatic failure from paracetamol
sections.6. Management – Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **A – Airway with C-spine protection:** GCS < 8 → intubate (RSI with etomidate + rocuronium; avoid suxamethonium if organophosphate risk). Protect airway from aspiration.
- **B – Breathing:** O₂ 15 L via non-rebreather; capnography to monitor respiratory rate. Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV push for opioid depression (repeat PRN; can give IM if no IV access). BUT beware: naloxone can precipitate withdrawal in dependent patients – use small increments (0.1 mg) titrated to respiratory rate > 10/min.
- **C – Circulation:** IV access (2 wide-bore). Fluids if hypotensive (crystalloid bolus 500 mL). Atropine for bradycardia with hypotension (organophosphate or opioid). Check ECG – treat arrhythmias per ALS.
- **D – Disability (decontamination):** Activated charcoal 50 g PO (or via NG) **only if** ingestion within 1 hour AND patient has intact airway (GCS > 14 or intubated). **Do not give** if ileus, caustic ingestion, or hydrocarbon ingestion (risk of aspiration pneumonitis).
- **E – Exposure:** Check for injection sites, toxidrome signs, temperature (hyperthermia in sympathomimetic overdose, hypothermia in opioid/ethanol). Glucose check – treat hypoglycaemia with 50 mL 50% dextrose IV.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Paracetamol overdose with NAC protocol:**
    - Loading dose: 150 mg/kg in 200 mL 5% dextrose IV over 1 hour
    - Second dose: 50 mg/kg in 500 mL 5% dextrose IV over 4 hours
    - Third dose: 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL 5% dextrose IV over 16 hours
    - Monitor transaminases, INR, U&E, lactate at 12, 24, 48 hours. If INR > 1.5 or rising ALT, continue NAC beyond 21-hour protocol until INR < 1.5 and ALT improving.
- **Opioid overdose:** Naloxone infusion (2/3 of dose that reversed toxicity per hour). Monitor for rebound sedation due to naloxone short half-life (20–30 min vs opioid 2–4 h). Consider long-acting opioid (methadone, buprenorphine) – may need prolonged monitoring.
- **Benzodiazepine overdose:** Supportive care – rarely requires flumazenil (only if iatrogenic over-sedation and no chronic use). **Pitfall:** flumazenil in mixed TCA/benzo OD can cause refractory seizures.
- **Anticholinergic delirium:** Physostigmine 0.5–2 mg IV slow push (contraindicated if QRS prolonged). Use cautiously – bradycardia, seizures.
- **Cholinergic crisis (organophosphate):** Atropine 1–2 mg IV every 5 min until secretions dry + pralidoxime 2 g IV over 30 min then 8mg/kg/h infusion.
- **Methanol/ethylene glycol:** Fomepizole 15 mg/kg IV loading then 10 mg/kg q12h for 4 doses (or ethanol infusion). Haemodialysis if severe metabolic acidosis (pH < 7.2), visual symptoms, or renal failure.
- **Salicylate:** Urinary alkalinisation (1.5 L 1.26% sodium bicarbonate over 2 h + 40 mg furosemide) if level < 700 mg/L (adult). Dialysis if level > 700, seizures, coma, pulmonary oedema.

**Long-term:**
- **Psychiatric assessment:** All intentional poisonings require mental health evaluation before discharge. Suicidality assessment, safeguarding children/home.
- **Antidote supplies:** Ensure pharmacy stocks key antidotes (naloxone, NAC, flumazenil, digoxin Fab, fomepizole). For rural/remote, have a ‘poisoning kit’ algorithm.
- **Patient education:** Avoid co-prescribing opioids/benzodiazepines; identify triggers.

**Common pitfalls:**
- ❌ **Giving AC late or in drowsy patient without airway protection** → aspiration pneumonitis, death
- ❌ **Using flumazenil in chronic benzo user** → withdrawal seizures
- ❌ **Not checking paracetamol level in every overdose** – even if patient denies, include it
- ❌ **Discharging after NAC protocol without checking INR and ALT** – delayed hepatotoxicity can occur
- ❌ **Assuming naloxone washout period is over** – patient may become sedated again; monitor 4–6 hours after last naloxone dose
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T: | Feature | Opioid Overdose | Sedative-Hypnotic (Benzodiazepine/EtOH) Overdose | Anticholinergic Syndrome | Metabolic Encephalopathy (Hypoglycaemia, Uraemia) | |---------|----------------|--------------------------------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------------| | **Pupils** | Pinpoint (miosis) | Normal to small (may be reactive) | Dilated (mydriasis) | Normal; may be sluggish | | **Respiratory rate** | Severely depressed (<8/min) | Depressed (10–12) with deep breathing (EtOH) | Normal to mild tachypnoea | Variable – Kussmaul if acidotic (uraemia) | | **Heart rate** | Bradycardia | Tachycardia (EtOH) or normal | Tachycardia | Tachycardia if sepsis; brady if uraemia/electrolyte | | **Temperature** | Hypothermic | Hypothermic | Hyperthermic (central blockade) | Normal or low (shock) | | **Skin** | Dry, cool | Flushed (EtOH) | Dry, flushed (hot as a hare) | Cool/diaphoretic (hypoglycaemia) | | **Response to naloxone** | ++ (rapid improvement in RR, GCS) | ± minimal (no significant effect) | None | None | | **ECG** | Sinus brady, normal QRS/QTc | Normal (unless EtOH-induced atrial fibrillation) | QRS normal, QTc prolonged possible (TCA – but TCA is anticholinergic) | Often normal; may show hyperkalaemia (peaked T waves) | | **Key lab** | Normal glucose, normal pH | Normal glucose, may have LFTs elevated (EtOH) | Normal; can have hyperthermia | Hypoglycaemia (<3.0 mmol/L), elevated urea/creatinine | | **Definitive test** | Urine opioids +; response to naloxone | Benzodiazepine screen; ethanol level | Physostigmine challenge (improves) | Dextrose corrects hypoglycaemia; BUN:Cr ratio | **Key distinguishing pattern:** Opioid OD → triad of miosis, bradypnoea, coma. Anticholinergic → “hot as a hare, red as a beet, dry as a bone, mad as a hatter”. Metabolic encephalopathy → features of underlying organ failure; no toxidrome.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Respiratory failure** (most lethal): missed opioid OD → progressive hypoxia, hypercapnia, cardiac arrest. Key sign: RR < 8/min, desaturation, CO₂ retention. **Do:** IV naloxone, BVM ventilate, call anaesthetics.

**Aspiration pneumonia:** vomiting + depressed airway reflexes (opioid, TCA, ethanol). Presents with fever, hypoxia, infiltrates on CXR. **Do:** antibiotics (amoxicillin/clavulanic acid), chest PT, high-flow nasal O₂.

**Torsades de pointes / ventricular arrhythmia:** QTc prolongation from TCA, antipsychotic (haloperidol), lithium, or drug interactions (e.g., methadone + citalopram). Key sign: QTc > 500 ms on ECG, syncope. **Do:** IV magnesium 2 g over 15 min, stop offending drug, telemetry.

**Acute liver failure (paracetamol):** Missed or delayed NAC → centrilobular necrosis, coagulopathy (INR > 1.5), encephalopathy, lactate > 3.5. King’s College criteria for transplant: pH < 7.3, or lactate > 3.5 + INR > 6.5 + creatinine > 300. **Late presentation:** patient may be well for 48h then jaundiced, encephalopathic.

**Acute kidney injury:** From myoglobinuria (sympathomimetic overdose, crush injury if prolonged coma), or direct nephrotoxicity (ethylene glycol, lithium, paracetamol hepatic failure → hepatorenal syndrome). Key sign: oliguria, rising creatinine, dark urine. **Do:** aggressive IV fluids, bicarb to alkalinise urine (if myoglobinuria), consider dialysis.

**Permanent neurological injury:** Carbon monoxide poisoning (missed) → basal ganglia necrosis, delayed encephalopathy. Ethylene glycol → cranial nerve palsies, blindness. Methyl alcohol → optic atrophy. **Key:** history of intoxication, metabolic acidosis, osmolar gap.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **TOXIDROME** (approach to any unknown ingestion):
   - T – Temperature (hypo/hyperthermia)
   - O – Observations (HR, BP, RR, O₂ sat)
   - X – Xenobiotic history (what, when, how much)
   - I – Inspection (needle marks, bottles, pupil size, skin moisture)
   - D – Depression of CNS (GCS)
   - R – Respiration (rate, pattern)
   - O – Odds (toxin-specific signs – seizures, arrhythmia)
   - M – Medical and psychiatric history (suicidality, drug dependence)
   - E – Electrocardiogram

2. **SLUDGE** (cholinergic crisis): Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI upset, Emesis.

3. **DEATH** (sympathomimetic overdose): Delirium, Excitation/agitation, Anxiety, Tachycardia, Hyperthermia → leads to rhabdomyolysis, seizures, stroke.

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Paracetamol is everywhere** – always run a level and start NAC if 150 mg/L at 4h, even if patient says “just a few tablets”. Don’t be misled by asymptomatic early course.
2. **Never give AC after 1 hour unless sustained-release preparation (e.g., venlafaxine, bupropion).**
3. **ANION GAP METABOLIC ACIDOSIS + OSMOLAR GAP = methanol or ethylene glycol** until proven otherwise. Send levels and start fomepizole empirically if high suspicion.
4. **“Wide QRS ≥ 100 ms”** – assume TCA overdose, give 1–2 mEq/kg sodium bicarbonate IV bolus (50 mL 8.4% bicarb over 1 min), repeat QRS check.
5. **Don’t forget glucose** – hypoglycaemia can mimic any intoxicated state and is fully reversible.
6. **Pregnancy test in all women of reproductive age** – paracetamol is safe in pregnancy but NAC dose is same; some drugs are teratogenic (e.g., warfarin, isotretinoin) or cause withdrawal in neonates.
7. **Co-ingestion rule** – if patient took more than one drug, the toxidrome may be mixed. Always check ECG, paracetamol level, and glucose in every overdose.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- ▪ *“But he’s only 4 hours post-ingestion, he looks fine”* – paracetamol looks fine until day 3; NAC works best if started <8h.
- ▪ *“She has pinpoint pupils, I’ll give naloxone, no need for ECG”* – she may also have TCA co-ingestion causing prolonged QRS.
- ▪ *“He drank ethanol, his acidosis is explained”* – ethanol typically causes mild ketoacidosis; if anion gap > 20, suspect co-ingestion of methanol or ethylene glycol.
- ▪ *“I’ll give flumazenil to wake him up”* – contraindicated if chronic benzo use (seizure) or mixed TCA (seizure).

Level above 150 mg/L at 4h → start NAC | | **Salicylate level** | Serum salicylate concentration | Suspicion of aspirin overdose (tinnitus, hyperventilation, metabolic acidosis) | Level > 30 mg/dL therapeutic; > 50 mg/dL toxic; > 90 mg/dL severe. Use nomogram for single acute ingestion | | **ABG** (arterial blood gas) | pH, bicarbonate, PaCO₂, lactate | Acid-base status; high anion gap metabolic acidosis suggests methanol, ethylene glycol, metformin, or salicylate | Anion gap = Na⁺ - (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻) > 12; osmolar gap = measured - calculated > 10 (methanol, ethylene glycol) | | **Coagulation screen** | PT/INR | Paracetamol hepatotoxicity – INR correlates with severity | INR > 1.5 at 24h suggests liver injury; > 2.0 at 72h is ominous | | **Glucose** | Blood sugar | Hypoglycemia from sulfonylurea, insulin, ethanol (also screen for Addison's in undifferentiated collapse) | Low glucose → IV dextrose; high glucose may be stress response | | **Pregnancy test** (urine/blood β-hCG) | +/− | Pregnancy alters management – teratogenicity, altered metabolism, UGI motility (delayed absorption) | Always check in women of childbearing age; affects decisions on AC, ICU transfer | | **Urine drug screen** (qualitative) | Presence of opioids, benzodiazepines, TCAs, cocaine metabolites | Adjunct when history is unreliable; may support toxidrome | Positive screen confirms exposure but does not quantitate; may miss synthetic opioids (fentanyl) | **Pattern-recognition single findings:** - **QRS > 100 ms with right axis deviation (R wave in aVR > 3 mm)** → TCA overdose until proven otherwise - **Anion gap metabolic acidosis + osmolar gap** → methanol/ethylene glycol (start fomepizole or ethanol) - **Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic acidosis** → salicylate toxicity (mixed acid-base disorder) - **INR > 4.0 + bilirubin > 300 μmol/L + lactic acidosis** → fulminant hepatic failure from paracetamol ### 6. Management – Approach & Reasoning **Immediate (first hour):** - **A – Airway with C-spine protection:** GCS < 8 → intubate (RSI with etomidate + rocuronium; avoid suxamethonium if organophosphate risk). Protect airway from aspiration. - **B – Breathing:** O₂ 15 L via non-rebreather; capnography to monitor respiratory rate. Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV push for opioid depression (repeat PRN; can give IM if no IV access). BUT beware: naloxone can precipitate withdrawal in dependent patients – use small increments (0.1 mg) titrated to respiratory rate > 10/min. - **C – Circulation:** IV access (2 wide-bore). Fluids if hypotensive (crystalloid bolus 500 mL). Atropine for bradycardia with hypotension (organophosphate or opioid). Check ECG – treat arrhythmias per ALS. - **D – Disability (decontamination):** Activated charcoal 50 g PO (or via NG) **only if** ingestion within 1 hour AND patient has intact airway (GCS > 14 or intubated). **Do not give** if ileus, caustic ingestion, or hydrocarbon ingestion (risk of aspiration pneumonitis). - **E – Exposure:** Check for injection sites, toxidrome signs, temperature (hyperthermia in sympathomimetic overdose, hypothermia in opioid/ethanol). Glucose check – treat hypoglycaemia with 50 mL 50% dextrose IV. **Ward (24–72 hours):** - **Paracetamol overdose with NAC protocol:** - Loading dose: 150 mg/kg in 200 mL 5% dextrose IV over 1 hour - Second dose: 50 mg/kg in 500 mL 5% dextrose IV over 4 hours - Third dose: 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL 5% dextrose IV over 16 hours - Monitor transaminases, INR, U&E, lactate at 12, 24, 48 hours. If INR > 1.5 or rising ALT, continue NAC beyond 21-hour protocol until INR < 1.5 and ALT improving. - **Opioid overdose:** Naloxone infusion (2/3 of dose that reversed toxicity per hour). Monitor for rebound sedation due to naloxone short half-life (20–30 min vs opioid 2–4 h). Consider long-acting opioid (methadone, buprenorphine) – may need prolonged monitoring. - **Benzodiazepine overdose:** Supportive care – rarely requires flumazenil (only if iatrogenic over-sedation and no chronic use). **Pitfall:** flumazenil in mixed TCA/benzo OD can cause refractory seizures. - **Anticholinergic delirium:** Physostigmine 0.5–2 mg IV slow push (contraindicated if QRS prolonged). Use cautiously – bradycardia, seizures. - **Cholinergic crisis (organophosphate):** Atropine 1–2 mg IV every 5 min until secretions dry + pralidoxime 2 g IV over 30 min then 8mg/kg/h infusion. - **Methanol/ethylene glycol:** Fomepizole 15 mg/kg IV loading then 10 mg/kg q12h for 4 doses (or ethanol infusion). Haemodialysis if severe metabolic acidosis (pH < 7.2), visual symptoms, or renal failure. - **Salicylate:** Urinary alkalinisation (1.5 L 1.26% sodium bicarbonate over 2 h + 40 mg furosemide) if level < 700 mg/L (adult). Dialysis if level > 700, seizures, coma, pulmonary oedema. **Long-term:** - **Psychiatric assessment:** All intentional poisonings require mental health evaluation before discharge. Suicidality assessment, safeguarding children/home. - **Antidote supplies:** Ensure pharmacy stocks key antidotes (naloxone, NAC, flumazenil, digoxin Fab, fomepizole). For rural/remote, have a ‘poisoning kit’ algorithm. - **Patient education:** Avoid co-prescribing opioids/benzodiazepines; identify triggers. **Common pitfalls:** - ❌ **Giving AC late or in drowsy patient without airway protection** → aspiration pneumonitis, death - ❌ **Using flumazenil in chronic benzo user** → withdrawal seizures - ❌ **Not checking paracetamol level in every overdose** – even if patient denies, include it - ❌ **Discharging after NAC protocol without checking INR and ALT** – delayed hepatotoxicity can occur - ❌ **Assuming naloxone washout period is over** – patient may become sedated again; monitor 4–6 hours after last naloxone dose ### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table | Feature | Opioid Overdose | Sedative-Hypnotic (Benzodiazepine/EtOH) Overdose | Anticholinergic Syndrome | Metabolic Encephalopathy (Hypoglycaemia, Uraemia) | |---------|----------------|--------------------------------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------------| | **Pupils** | Pinpoint (miosis) | Normal to small (may be reactive) | Dilated (mydriasis) | Normal; may be sluggish | | **Respiratory rate** | Severely depressed (<8/min) | Depressed (10–12) with deep breathing (EtOH) | Normal to mild tachypnoea | Variable – Kussmaul if acidotic (uraemia) | | **Heart rate** | Bradycardia | Tachycardia (EtOH) or normal | Tachycardia | Tachycardia if sepsis; brady if uraemia/electrolyte | | **Temperature** | Hypothermic | Hypothermic | Hyperthermic (central blockade) | Normal or low (shock) | | **Skin** | Dry, cool | Flushed (EtOH) | Dry, flushed (hot as a hare) | Cool/diaphoretic (hypoglycaemia) | | **Response to naloxone** | ++ (rapid improvement in RR, GCS) | ± minimal (no significant effect) | None | None | | **ECG** | Sinus brady, normal QRS/QTc | Normal (unless EtOH-induced atrial fibrillation) | QRS normal, QTc prolonged possible (TCA – but TCA is anticholinergic) | Often normal; may show hyperkalaemia (peaked T waves) | | **Key lab** | Normal glucose, normal pH | Normal glucose, may have LFTs elevated (EtOH) | Normal; can have hyperthermia | Hypoglycaemia (<3.0 mmol/L), elevated urea/creatinine | | **Definitive test** | Urine opioids +; response to naloxone | Benzodiazepine screen; ethanol level | Physostigmine challenge (improves) | Dextrose corrects hypoglycaemia; BUN:Cr ratio | **Key distinguishing pattern:** Opioid OD → triad of miosis, bradypnoea, coma. Anticholinergic → “hot as a hare, red as a beet, dry as a bone, mad as a hatter”. Metabolic encephalopathy → features of underlying organ failure; no toxidrome. ### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It? **Respiratory failure** (most lethal): missed opioid OD → progressive hypoxia, hypercapnia, cardiac arrest. Key sign: RR < 8/min, desaturation, CO₂ retention. **Do:** IV naloxone, BVM ventilate, call anaesthetics. **Aspiration pneumonia:** vomiting + depressed airway reflexes (opioid, TCA, ethanol). Presents with fever, hypoxia, infiltrates on CXR. **Do:** antibiotics (amoxicillin/clavulanic acid), chest PT, high-flow nasal O₂. **Torsades de pointes / ventricular arrhythmia:** QTc prolongation from TCA, antipsychotic (haloperidol), lithium, or drug interactions (e.g., methadone + citalopram). Key sign: QTc > 500 ms on ECG, syncope. **Do:** IV magnesium 2 g over 15 min, stop offending drug, telemetry. **Acute liver failure (paracetamol):** Missed or delayed NAC → centrilobular necrosis, coagulopathy (INR > 1.5), encephalopathy, lactate > 3.5. King’s College criteria for transplant: pH < 7.3, or lactate > 3.5 + INR > 6.5 + creatinine > 300. **Late presentation:** patient may be well for 48h then jaundiced, encephalopathic. **Acute kidney injury:** From myoglobinuria (sympathomimetic overdose, crush injury if prolonged coma), or direct nephrotoxicity (ethylene glycol, lithium, paracetamol hepatic failure → hepatorenal syndrome). Key sign: oliguria, rising creatinine, dark urine. **Do:** aggressive IV fluids, bicarb to alkalinise urine (if myoglobinuria), consider dialysis. **Permanent neurological injury:** Carbon monoxide poisoning (missed) → basal ganglia necrosis, delayed encephalopathy. Ethylene glycol → cranial nerve palsies, blindness. Methyl alcohol → optic atrophy. **Key:** history of intoxication, metabolic acidosis, osmolar gap. ### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls **Mnemonics:** 1. **TOXIDROME** (approach to any unknown ingestion): - T – Temperature (hypo/hyperthermia) - O – Observations (HR, BP, RR, O₂ sat) - X – Xenobiotic history (what, when, how much) - I – Inspection (needle marks, bottles, pupil size, skin moisture) - D – Depression of CNS (GCS) - R – Respiration (rate, pattern) - O – Odds (toxin-specific signs – seizures, arrhythmia) - M – Medical and psychiatric history (suicidality, drug dependence) - E – Electrocardiogram 2. **SLUDGE** (cholinergic crisis): Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI upset, Emesis. 3. **DEATH** (sympathomimetic overdose): Delirium, Excitation/agitation, Anxiety, Tachycardia, Hyperthermia → leads to rhabdomyolysis, seizures, stroke. **Clinical pearls:** 1. **Paracetamol is everywhere** – always run a level and start NAC if 150 mg/L at 4h, even if patient says “just a few tablets”. Don’t be misled by asymptomatic early course. 2. **Never give AC after 1 hour unless sustained-release preparation (e.g., venlafaxine, bupropion).** 3. **ANION GAP METABOLIC ACIDOSIS + OSMOLAR GAP = methanol or ethylene glycol** until proven otherwise. Send levels and start fomepizole empirically if high suspicion. 4. **“Wide QRS ≥ 100 ms”** – assume TCA overdose, give 1–2 mEq/kg sodium bicarbonate IV bolus (50 mL 8.4% bicarb over 1 min), repeat QRS check. 5. **Don’t forget glucose** – hypoglycaemia can mimic any intoxicated state and is fully reversible. 6. **Pregnancy test in all women of reproductive age** – paracetamol is safe in pregnancy but NAC dose is same; some drugs are teratogenic (e.g., warfarin, isotretinoin) or cause withdrawal in neonates. 7. **Co-ingestion rule** – if patient took more than one drug, the toxidrome may be mixed. Always check ECG, paracetamol level, and glucose in every overdose. **Common exam / ward mistakes:** - ▪ *“But he’s only 4 hours post-ingestion, he looks fine”* – paracetamol looks fine until day 3; NAC works best if started <8h. - ▪ *“She has pinpoint pupils, I’ll give naloxone, no need for ECG”* – she may also have TCA co-ingestion causing prolonged QRS. - ▪ *“He drank ethanol, his acidosis is explained”* – ethanol typically causes mild ketoacidosis; if anion gap > 20, suspect co-ingestion of methanol or ethylene glycol. - ▪ *“I’ll give flumazenil to wake him up”* – contraindicated if chronic benzo use (seizure) or mixed TCA (seizure). ### 10. Escalation Criteria **When to call senior (
